subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
intro: Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from a single Euler step, through the two-slope second-order methods (improved Euler, modified Euler, and Heun), into the four-stage RK4 step, and close with a method comparison and an adaptive-stepping capstone. Throughout, take $\frac{dy}{dt}=f(t,y)$ with step size $h$. Solutions follow in Part III.

takeaway.title: Unit 5 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
Accuracy is bought with slopes per step. \highlight{Euler} uses one
(first order); \highlight{improved/modified Euler and Heun} average
two (second order); \highlight{RK4} weights four as
$\highlight{\tfrac{h}{6}(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)}$ (fourth order). On
$y'=t+y$ over a single $h=0.2$ step, the errors fall from $\sim 10^{-2}$ to
$\sim 10^{-6}$ across the ladder --- and \highlight{adaptive stepping}
spends that accuracy budget only where the solution demands it.

problems.problem: Write the Euler update formula, then take one Euler step for $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = t + y$ with $y(0) = 1$ and $h = 0.2$. Report $y_1$ and $t_1$.
problems.solution:
The Euler update is
$y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(t_n,y_n)$. With $f(t,y)=t+y$, the starting slope is
$f(0,1) = 0 + 1 = 1$:
\[ \work{y_1 = 1 + 0.2\cdot 1 = 1.2, \qquad t_1 = 0.2.} \]

\medskip

problems.problem: Continue from Problem 1 and take a \emph{second} Euler step to find $y_2$ at $t_2 = 0.4$.
State the local and global truncation error orders of Euler's method, and the factor by which the global error changes when $h$ is halved.
problems.solution:
A single Euler step drops the
$\tfrac{h^{2}}{2}y''$ term, so the \strong{local} truncation error is $O(h^{2})$.
Accumulated over $\sim 1/h$ steps, the \strong{global} error is $O(h)$, making
Euler a first-order method. Halving $h$ therefore
\[ \work{\text{cuts the global error by about a factor of } 2.} \]

\medskip

problems.problem: Take one \emph{improved Euler} step for $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = t + y$ with $y(0) = 1$ and $h = 0.2$. Show $k_1$, $k_2$, and $y_1$.
problems.solution:
Improved Euler uses
$k_1 = f(t_n,y_n)$ and $k_2 = f(t_n+h,\,y_n+hk_1)$, then averages:
\[ \work{k_1 = f(0,1) = 1, \qquad k_2 = f(0.2,\;1 + 0.2\cdot 1) = f(0.2,\,1.2) = 1.4.} \]
\[ \work{y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.2}{2}\left(1 + 1.4\right) = 1 + 0.1\cdot 2.4 = 1.24.} \]

\medskip

problems.problem: Take one \emph{modified Euler} (midpoint) step for the same problem, $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = t + y$, $y(0)=1$, $h = 0.2$. Show $k_1$, $k_2$, and $y_1$.
problems.solution:
Modified Euler reads the slope at the
midpoint after a half-step prediction:
\[ \work{k_1 = f(0,1) = 1, \qquad k_2 = f\!\left(0.1,\; 1 + 0.1\cdot 1\right) = f(0.1,\,1.1) = 1.2.} \]
\[ \work{y_1 = 1 + h\,k_2 = 1 + 0.2\cdot 1.2 = 1.24.} \]
Here both second-order methods agree to the digits shown.

\medskip

problems.problem: Write Heun's predictor--corrector formulas and explain in one line why the method is second order.
problems.solution:
Heun predicts with a full Euler step and
corrects with the trapezoidal average of the endpoint slopes:
\[ \work{\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n,y_n), \qquad
   y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\big[f(t_n,y_n) + f(t_{n+1},\tilde{y}_{n+1})\big].} \]
Averaging the two endpoint slopes restores the leading curvature term that plain
Euler discards, so the global error is $O(h^{2})$ --- second order.

\medskip

problems.problem: For a general $f(t,y)$, write the four RK4 stage slopes $k_1,k_2,k_3,k_4$ and the final RK4 update for $y_{n+1}$.
problems.solution:
The classical RK4 stages and update are
\[ \work{k_1 = f(t_n,y_n),} \]
\[ \work{k_2 = f\!\left(t_n+\tfrac{h}{2},\, y_n+\tfrac{h}{2}k_1\right),
   \qquad k_3 = f\!\left(t_n+\tfrac{h}{2},\, y_n+\tfrac{h}{2}k_2\right),} \]
\[ \work{k_4 = f\!\left(t_n+h,\, y_n+h k_3\right),} \]
\[ \work{y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6}\left(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4\right).} \]

\medskip

problems.problem: Take one RK4 step for $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = y$ with $y(0) = 1$ and $h = 0.1$. Compute $y_1$ and compare it to $e^{0.1}$.
Rank Euler, improved/modified Euler, Heun, and RK4 by order of accuracy, and give the factor by which each method's global error drops when $h$ is halved.
problems.solution:
Ranked by order of accuracy:
\[
\work{
\begin{array}{lll}
\text{Euler} & \text{1st order } O(h) & \text{halving } h \Rightarrow \div 2\\
\text{Improved / Modified Euler} & \text{2nd order } O(h^{2}) & \text{halving } h \Rightarrow \div 4\\
\text{Heun} & \text{2nd order } O(h^{2}) & \text{halving } h \Rightarrow \div 4\\
\text{RK4} & \text{4th order } O(h^{4}) & \text{halving } h \Rightarrow \div 16
\end{array}}
\]

\medskip

problems.problem: Explain how an adaptive solver uses two estimates of \emph{different} order to decide whether to shrink or grow the step size $h$.
problems.solution:
An adaptive solver advances each step two
ways of \emph{different} order --- for example an embedded RK4/RK5 pair, or one
full step compared against two half steps. The difference between the two
estimates approximates the local error. If that estimate \strong{exceeds} the
tolerance, the solver \strong{shrinks} $h$ and retries the step; if it sits
comfortably \strong{under} the tolerance, the solver \strong{grows} $h$ for the
next step, so the work concentrates where the solution changes fastest.

\medskip

problems.problem: \strong{(Capstone)} Take one RK4 step for $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = t + y$ with $y(0) = 1$ and $h = 0.2$. Compute $y_1$, then compare it against the Euler result (Problem 1) and the improved Euler result (Problem 4) using the exact solution $y = 2e^{t} - t - 1$.
